notes: Run 2026-06-26 (category 1 of 2). Active count was 44 — thinnest non-geography eligible category (arts/science/music/words_language all had open PRs). Under-weight tiers: $200 (3 active) and $1000 (5 active). Pack weighted accordingly: 3x $200, 3x $400, 6x $600, 1x $800, 2x $1000. Gaps filled: Greek Mythology (Heracles, Zeus, Perseus, Pegasus), Bible (John the Baptist, Sermon on the Mount, Nimrod), Philosophers (Descartes, Plato, Aquinas), Philosophical Schools (Existentialism), Religious Texts & Terms (Book of Mormon), Symbols/Rituals (Tree of Life), World Religions (Islam, Saint Christopher). Hard tail: Nimrod ($1000, biblical figure whose name ironically became slang for fool) + Pegasus ($1000, oblique from Hippocrene/thunderbolts angle).

questions.clue: The greatest hero of ancient Greece, this son of Zeus and the mortal Alcmene was renowned for extraordinary strength and performed twelve legendary labors imposed as penance for a fit of madness sent upon him by Hera.

citations.title: Heracles
citations.snippet: Heracles, born Alcaeus or Alcides, was a divine hero in Greek mythology, the son of Zeus and Alcmene, and the foster son of Amphitryon. He was a descendant of Perseus, another son of Zeus.

questions.clue: Supreme king of the Greek gods, this deity ruled from Mount Olympus with the thunderbolt and eagle as his symbols and was later identified by the Romans with their chief god Jupiter.

citations.title: Zeus
citations.snippet: Zeus is the supreme deity of the Greek pantheon. He is a sky and thunder god in ancient Greek religion and mythology, who rules as king of the gods on Mount Olympus.

questions.clue: The world's second-largest religious population after Christianity, this Abrahamic faith's followers observe Five Pillars including the hajj pilgrimage to Mecca and the five daily prayers directed toward the holy city.

citations.title: Islam
citations.snippet: Islam is an Abrahamic monotheistic religion based on the Quran and the teachings of Muhammad. The religion has an estimated 2 billion worldwide adherents, called Muslims. Islam is the world's second-largest religious population after Christ

questions.clue: This first-century Jewish preacher, active near the Jordan River, announced a messianic figure greater than himself, performed a ritual immersion of Jesus, and was later beheaded at the order of Herod Antipas for denouncing the ruler's unlawful remarriage.

citations.title: John the Baptist
citations.snippet: John the Baptist was a Jewish preacher active in the area of the Jordan River in the early first century AD. He is also known as Saint John the Forerunner in Eastern Orthodoxy, Eastern Catholicism, and Oriental Orthodoxy, Saint John the Imm

questions.clue: The first of five major discourse sections in the Gospel of Matthew, this celebrated collection of Jesus's moral teachings spans chapters 5 through 7 and has been one of the most widely quoted passages in all the Gospels.

citations.title: Sermon on the Mount
citations.snippet: The Sermon on the Mount is a collection of sayings spoken by Jesus of Nazareth found in chapters 5, 6, and 7 of the Gospel of Matthew that summarizes his moral teachings. It is the first of five discourses in the Gospel and has been one of

questions.clue: Often called the father of modern philosophy, this 17th-century French mathematician coined the axiom 'I think, therefore I am' and fused algebra with geometry into analytic geometry — a field that still bears his name.

citations.title: René Descartes
citations.snippet: René Descartes was a French philosopher, scientist, logician, and mathematician, widely considered a seminal figure in the emergence of modern philosophy and science during the Renaissance era. Mathematics was paramount to his method of inq

questions.clue: Alfred North Whitehead characterized the entire European philosophical tradition as 'a series of footnotes' to this Athenian's work; a student of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle, he founded the Academy and articulated the Theory of Forms.

citations.title: Plato
citations.snippet: Plato was an ancient Greek philosopher of Classical Athens who is most commonly considered the foundational thinker of the Western philosophical tradition. An innovator of the literary dialogue and dialectic forms, Plato influenced all the

questions.clue: This 13th-century Italian Dominican priest synthesized Aristotle's philosophy with Catholic doctrine in his vast unfinished Summa Theologiae and earned the church honorifics 'Doctor Angelicus' and 'Doctor Communis.'

citations.title: Thomas Aquinas
citations.snippet: Thomas Aquinas was an Italian Dominican friar and priest, theologian, and philosopher. He is considered one of the most influential thinkers in the history of Catholic theology and Western philosophy.

questions.clue: First published in 1830 by Joseph Smith, this sacred scripture describes an appearance of Jesus in the Americas after his resurrection and serves as the 'keystone' of the Latter Day Saint movement.

citations.title: Book of Mormon
citations.snippet: The Book of Mormon is a religious text of the Latter Day Saint movement, first published in 1830 by Joseph Smith as The Book of Mormon: An Account Written by the Hand of Mormon upon Plates Taken from the Plates of Nephi.

questions.clue: Associated with 20th-century thinkers including Sartre, Heidegger, de Beauvoir, and Camus, this philosophical movement holds that personal freedom and authentic choice are essential to creating meaning amid the apparent absurdity of the human condition.

citations.title: Existentialism
citations.snippet: Existentialism is a family of philosophical views and inquiry that explore the human individual's struggle to lead an authentic life despite the apparent absurdity or incomprehensibility of existence. In examining meaning, purpose, and valu

questions.clue: In Eastern Byzantine iconography, this legendary martyr was depicted with a dog's head; Western hagiography reimagines him as a giant who ferried travelers across a river and found one small passenger growing unbearably heavy — a child who then revealed his divine identity on the far bank.

citations.title: Saint Christopher
citations.snippet: Saint Christopher (Greek: Ἅγιος Χριστόφορος, romanized: Hágios Christóphoros, lit. 'Christ-bearer'; Latin: Sanctus Christophorus), also called Christopher of Lycia (Lycea), is a legendary figure venerated by several Christian denominations

questions.clue: Found across world traditions — from the sacred pillar of wood in Genesis's Garden of Eden to the Norse Yggdrasil — this universal cosmic archetype represents the connection between the underworld, the earth, and the heavens as the axis of all creation.

citations.title: Tree of life
citations.snippet: The tree of life is a fundamental archetype in many of the world's mythological, religious, and philosophical traditions. It is closely related to the concept of the sacred tree. The tree of the knowledge of good and evil and the tree of li

questions.clue: The son of Zeus and the mortal Danaë, this demigod was the greatest monster-slayer of the generation before Heracles; he rescued the princess Andromeda after vanquishing the sea monster Cetus, which had been sent to devour her coastal city.

citations.title: Perseus
citations.snippet: In Greek mythology, Perseus is the legendary founder of the Perseid dynasty. He was, alongside Cadmus and Bellerophon, the greatest Greek hero and slayer of monsters before the days of Heracles. He beheaded the Gorgon Medusa for Polydectes

questions.clue: Described in Genesis as a 'mighty hunter before the Lord,' this great-grandson of Noah founded a kingdom in Shinar and is linked by tradition to the Tower of Babel; in modern slang, his name has come to mean a bumbling fool — a complete inversion of its original sense.

citations.title: Nimrod
citations.snippet: Nimrod is a biblical figure mentioned in the Book of Genesis and the Books of Chronicles. The son of Cush and thus the great-grandson of Noah, Nimrod was described as a king in the land of Shinar. The Bible states that he was "a mighty hunt

questions.clue: Sired by the sea-god and born when a Greek hero slew the Gorgon — his blood mixing with sea-foam — this divine stallion stamped his hoof on Mount Helicon to create the Muses' sacred spring Hippocrene and later bore Zeus's thunderbolts to Olympus.

citations.title: Pegasus
citations.snippet: Pegasus is a winged horse in Greek mythology, usually depicted as a white stallion. He was sired by Poseidon, in his role as horse-god, and foaled by the Gorgon Medusa. Pegasus was the brother of Chrysaor, both born from Medusa's blood when
